questions.question_text: What studies have attempted to empirically separate ART (attention fatigue restoration) from SRT (physiological stress recovery) pathways in the same experimental design?
questions.description: This is the central confound in the field. Finding a decisive dissociation study would be a major contribution.
search_hints: ART SRT dissociation
attention restoration stress recovery compare
Kaplan Ulrich same study mechanism

questions.question_text: What is the evidence that natural sounds (birdsong, flowing water) reduce stress or improve cognitive performance compared to urban noise?
questions.description: Acoustic biophilia — an important and growing literature.
search_hints: birdsong stress reduction
nature sounds cognitive performance
acoustic environment wellbeing

questions.question_text: How does noise from open-plan offices affect cognitive performance and stress, and what acoustic interventions most reduce the harm?
questions.description: Highly practical question with a large applied literature.
search_hints: open plan office noise cognitive
acoustic privacy office
speech intelligibility distraction

questions.question_text: What evidence from large-scale epidemiological studies shows that neighbourhood walkability scores—combining land use mix, street connectivity, and residential density—independently predict objectively measured physical activity and cardiovascular health outcomes after adjusting for individual-level confounders?
questions.description: Walkability and physical activity — well-studied but confounding is a persistent challenge.
search_hints: Neighbourhood walkability score physical activity cardiovascular health evidence epidemiological
Do walkable neighbourhoods cause higher physical activity and better health after controlling for confounders?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that architectural interventions improving stairwell attractiveness—including natural light, artwork, music, or point-of-decision prompts—meaningfully increase stair use over elevator or escalator use in real workplace or public-building settings?
questions.description: Active design in buildings — point-of-choice prompts and aesthetic stairwells.
search_hints: Stairwell design attractiveness stair use elevator preference evidence active design
Do attractive naturally lit stairwells increase stair climbing compared to elevators evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that nature-integrated school play environments—including loose parts, naturalistic landforms, vegetation, and water features—increase the vigour and duration of children's physical activity at recess compared to conventional equipment-focused playgrounds?
questions.description: Nature play and physical activity — connects landscape design to public health.
search_hints: Nature play schoolyard physical activity recess vigour evidence compared traditional playground
Do naturalistic school playgrounds with loose parts and vegetation increase children's physical activity?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that proximity to quality urban parks—controlling for socioeconomic status, age, and self-selection—independently predicts objectively measured moderate-to-vigorous physical activity levels in adults and children?
questions.description: Park access and physical activity — self-selection bias is a major challenge in this literature.
search_hints: Urban park proximity physical activity adults children evidence controlling self-selection socioeconomic
Does living near parks independently increase physical activity controlling for income and self-selection?

questions.question_text: What empirical evidence supports the claim that natural environments restore directed attention better than urban environments?
questions.description: Tests Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) core ART prediction. Good starter question with broad literature.
search_hints: attention restoration theory
directed attention fatigue
nature restorative
Kaplan involuntary attention

questions.question_text: What studies distinguish fascination as the active mechanism in attention restoration from mere relaxation or positive affect?
questions.description: Targets the mechanism question — does fascination per se do the work, or is it confounded with mood?
search_hints: fascination attention restoration
involuntary attention mechanism
ART mechanism isolation

questions.question_text: Can built indoor environments (offices, classrooms, hospitals) produce attention restoration effects comparable to outdoor nature?
questions.description: Tests ART transferability to built interiors — especially relevant for biophilic design.
search_hints: indoor nature restorative
biophilic design attention
window view restoration office

questions.question_text: What experimental evidence supports the biophilia hypothesis — an innate human affinity for natural environments — as opposed to learned preference?
questions.description: Tests Wilson (1984) innate claim. Cross-cultural and infant studies most relevant.
search_hints: biophilia hypothesis evidence
innate nature preference
cross-cultural biophilia Wilson

questions.question_text: What design features — plants, water, natural light, natural materials — show the strongest evidence for reducing stress or improving wellbeing in offices?
questions.description: Applied biophilic design question. Connects theory to practice.
search_hints: biophilic design office wellbeing
indoor plants stress reduction
natural light workplace health

questions.question_text: How well do findings from Japanese forest-bathing (shinrin-yoku) studies generalise to Western or urban populations?
questions.description: Population transfer question — directly relevant to the K-ATLAS corpus gap around East Asian study cohorts.
search_hints: shinrin-yoku Western populations
forest bathing generalisation
cross-cultural nature stress Japan

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that contact with biodiverse natural environments—soil microbiota, plant surfaces, woodland air—affects the human gut and skin microbiome in ways that subsequently modulate immune function, inflammation markers, and mental health via the gut-brain axis?
questions.description: The microbiome mechanism for nature-mental health links — an emerging but rapidly growing literature.
search_hints: Nature contact microbiome gut brain axis immune function mental health evidence
Does contact with natural environments change the microbiome in ways that improve mental health evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that structured nature-based mindfulness programmes—including forest bathing with guided meditative attention—produce greater reductions in stress, anxiety, and rumination than equivalent time spent in nature without guided mindfulness, or mindfulness practice in indoor settings?
questions.description: Nature + mindfulness interaction — is the combination superadditive?
search_hints: Nature mindfulness forest bathing guided meditation stress anxiety rumination evidence
Is mindfulness combined with nature more effective than nature alone or indoor mindfulness evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that restorative and stress-reducing effects of natural environments vary significantly across seasons—with winter bare-canopy urban parks producing weaker effects than the same parks in summer—and what design or programming interventions offset seasonal degradation of restorative quality?
questions.description: Seasonal variation in restorative environments — a practical question for park design and management.
search_hints: Seasonal variation restorative environment winter summer nature effect evidence
Are parks and natural environments less restorative in winter compared to summer evidence?

questions.question_text: What experimental evidence supports specific colour recommendations (e.g. blue for focus, green for calm) in educational or healthcare interior design?
questions.description: Applied colour psychology — a domain where popular claims often outrun the evidence.
search_hints: colour psychology built environment
blue green mood productivity
color classroom healthcare evidence

questions.question_text: What experimental evidence shows that walking in natural environments—as opposed to equivalent urban walks or treadmill walking—specifically enhances scores on divergent and convergent creative thinking tests, and what mechanism—ART, positive affect, or reduced rumination—best explains the effect?
questions.description: Walking, nature, and creativity — the mechanism is genuinely disputed.
search_hints: Walking nature divergent thinking creativity mechanism ART positive affect evidence
Does walking in nature enhance creative thinking more than urban walking and why evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that moderate ambient noise—approximately 65–70 dB, as found in coffee shops—enhances performance on divergent thinking tasks compared to silence, and how does this relate to the effect of noise on analytical versus creative work?
questions.description: The Starbucks effect — evidence for and against creativity-friendly noise levels.
search_hints: Ambient noise 70 dB coffee shop creative thinking divergent evidence
Does moderate background noise like a coffee shop enhance divergent creative thinking evidence?

questions.question_text: What experimental evidence tests the prediction that high-ceiling spaces promote abstract, categorical, and creative thinking while low-ceiling spaces promote concrete, detail-focused thinking, and how replicable is the original Meyers-Levy finding?
questions.description: Ceiling height and abstraction — the replication record matters for K-ATLAS credence.
search_hints: Ceiling height abstract thinking creativity Meyers-Levy replication evidence
Does ceiling height affect abstract thinking and creativity replication evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that creative professionals in naturally lit studio or office environments—compared to artificially lit equivalent spaces—produce higher originality ratings, more varied outputs, and report higher creative self-efficacy?
questions.description: Natural light and creative performance — extends daylighting evidence beyond productivity.
search_hints: Natural daylight creative studio originality creative performance evidence
Does natural light in design studios improve creative output quality compared to artificial light evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that perceived residential crowding—independent of objective density—is associated with elevated cortisol, disrupted sleep, reduced cognitive performance, and impaired children's reading scores?
questions.description: Perceived vs. objective crowding distinction — critical for understanding equity effects of housing.
search_hints: Perceived crowding residential cortisol sleep cognitive performance children evidence
Is perceived crowding independent of objective density associated with stress and cognitive impairment evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the degree of acoustic and visual privacy available in workplace settings independently affects willingness to disclose sensitive information, perceived psychological safety, and quality of interpersonal conversations?
questions.description: Privacy and trust — connects spatial design to organisational psychology and safety culture.
search_hints: Acoustic privacy workplace psychological safety trust sensitive conversation evidence
Does acoustic privacy in offices affect trust and willingness to share sensitive information evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that public transport crowding—measured by passenger density at peak hours—increases salivary cortisol, perceived interpersonal threat, and subsequent social withdrawal behaviour compared to uncrowded journeys of equal length?
questions.description: Transport crowding and social sequelae — bridges environmental psychology and public health.
search_hints: Public transport crowding cortisol stress social withdrawal evidence commuting
Does crowded public transport increase stress hormones and social avoidance evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that designing open-plan offices with semi-private territorial zones—including visual barriers, acoustic enclosures, and clearly demarcated personal areas—reduces reports of territorial conflict, perceived crowding, and job strain?
questions.description: Territoriality in open offices — a design strategy to restore personal space without physical walls.
search_hints: Territorial design open plan office semi-private zones territorial conflict crowding evidence
Do semi-private zones and territorial cues in open offices reduce conflict and perceived crowding?

questions.question_text: How does cultural background shape preferences for natural versus built environments, and what are the implications for universal design guidelines?
questions.description: Cross-cultural validity of Western-derived environmental psychology principles.
search_hints: cross-cultural nature preference
biophilic design culture
cultural variation restorative environment

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that daylighting strategies in buildings reduce seasonal mood changes or depressive symptoms beyond clinical SAD populations?
questions.description: Extends the SAD literature to general populations — important for building code and design.
search_hints: daylighting seasonal depression
light therapy SAD generalisation
window daylighting mood nonclinical

questions.question_text: What peer-reviewed evidence—including large-scale longitudinal studies such as the Heschong Mahone daylighting studies—shows that classroom daylighting quality predicts reading and mathematics achievement in elementary school students after controlling for socioeconomic factors?
questions.description: The Heschong Mahone studies are the most cited classroom daylighting evidence — but have been contested on methodology.
search_hints: Classroom daylighting natural light student achievement reading mathematics evidence
Does more daylight in classrooms improve student academic performance Heschong Mahone evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that excessive reverberation and background noise levels in primary school classrooms—above the recommended signal-to-noise ratio of +15 dB—impair phonological processing, reading fluency, and language development in young children?
questions.description: Acoustic standards for classrooms — the evidence base for ANSI S12.60 classroom acoustics guidelines.
search_hints: Classroom noise reverberation phonological processing reading language development children evidence
How does acoustic quality in schools affect language development and reading in children?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that access to nature-rich schoolyards—with trees, gardens, and unstructured natural materials—improves children's attention, reduces behavioural problems, and supports executive function compared to conventional paved playgrounds?
questions.description: Schoolyard nature and child development — connects ART to educational outcomes.
search_hints: Nature-rich schoolyard attention behavioural problems executive function children evidence
Does access to green naturalistic schoolyards improve attention and reduce conduct problems in children?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that elevated CO2 concentrations—above 1000 ppm—in poorly ventilated classrooms impair students' cognitive performance on decision-making and information-use tasks, and what ventilation interventions most effectively reduce these concentrations?
questions.description: CO2 and cognition in classrooms — a surprisingly strong effect with very practical design implications.
search_hints: CO2 concentration classroom ventilation student cognitive performance evidence
Does high carbon dioxide in poorly ventilated classrooms impair student thinking and decision making?

questions.question_text: What evidence from controlled comparisons shows that flexible classroom furniture arrangements—including moveable seating, varied table configurations, and maker-space zones—improve student engagement, collaborative behaviour, and learning outcomes compared to fixed row seating?
questions.description: Flexible furniture and active learning — increasingly popular but evidence quality varies widely.
search_hints: Flexible classroom furniture learning outcomes engagement evidence comparison traditional
Do flexible moveable classroom furniture arrangements improve student engagement and achievement?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the physical condition and maintenance quality of school buildings—including roof leaks, broken heating systems, and poor lighting—independently affects student achievement, absenteeism, and teacher retention after controlling for school demographics?
questions.description: School building condition and equity — a policy-relevant question with strong implications for capital investment.
search_hints: School building condition maintenance quality student achievement teacher retention evidence
Does poor school building condition affect student outcomes and teacher retention independently of demographics?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that biophilic classroom elements—including indoor plants, nature views, natural materials, and daylight—reduce cortisol levels and attentional difficulties specifically in children diagnosed with ADHD or attention difficulties?
questions.description: Biophilic design + ADHD — a high-value clinical translation of ART predictions.
search_hints: Biophilic classroom design ADHD attention cortisol children evidence plants daylight
Do nature elements in classrooms specifically help children with ADHD maintain attention evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the psychosocial benefits of biophilic design—including stress reduction, improved mood, enhanced social contact, and cognitive restoration—differ systematically by income level, racial background, or housing tenure, suggesting that urban green inequity is also a health equity issue?
questions.description: Green space equity — the distribution of biophilic benefits across social groups.
search_hints: Biophilic design benefits income race housing inequality evidence equity green space
Do the mental health benefits of nature and green space differ by income or race evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence from longitudinal studies spanning five or more years shows that sustained residential exposure to high-quality biophilic environments—compared to nature-deprived urban settings—produces cumulative reductions in allostatic load, chronic stress markers, and cardiovascular disease risk?
questions.description: Long-term cumulative biophilic benefit — the strongest possible argument for biophilic design investment.
search_hints: Longitudinal biophilic environment exposure allostatic load chronic stress cardiovascular evidence five years
What longitudinal evidence shows cumulative health benefits of living near nature over multiple years?

questions.question_text: What evidence supports Richard Louv's nature deficit disorder hypothesis—that children growing up with limited access to natural environments show measurable increases in attention difficulties, anxiety, and sensory processing problems compared to children with regular nature contact?
questions.description: Nature deficit disorder — widely cited in public discourse but empirical evidence is contested.
search_hints: Nature deficit disorder children attention anxiety sensory problems evidence Richard Louv
Is there empirical evidence that children with limited nature contact have more attention and anxiety problems?

questions.question_text: What is the evidence for a Goldilocks optimal fractal dimension (approximately D=1.3–1.5) in visual patterns that reduces psychophysiological stress?
questions.description: Tests the D=1.3-1.5 finding from Taylor et al. and related work.
search_hints: fractal dimension stress reduction
Taylor fractal 1.3
visual complexity psychophysiology

questions.question_text: Is the stress-reducing effect of fractal patterns explained by fractal self-similarity per se, or by spatial frequency and luminance contrast?
questions.description: Tests the mechanism question raised in the fractal dimension literature. A genuine open question.
search_hints: fractal self-similarity mechanism
spatial frequency fractal
luminance contrast visual preference

questions.question_text: What peer-reviewed evidence shows that single-patient hospital rooms—compared to shared multi-bed wards—reduce healthcare-acquired infection rates, improve sleep quality, and accelerate post-surgical recovery?
questions.description: Single-patient room evidence base — a major capital investment decision driven by limited evidence.
search_hints: Single patient hospital rooms infection rates sleep recovery evidence systematic review
Do single hospital rooms versus shared wards improve patient outcomes peer-reviewed evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that dynamic circadian lighting protocols in intensive care units—designed to preserve melatonin rhythms through graduated blue-light exposure across the 24-hour cycle—reduce ICU delirium and accelerate patient discharge?
questions.description: ICU delirium and lighting — one of the highest-stakes biophilic/circadian design questions.
search_hints: Circadian lighting ICU delirium reduction melatonin evidence randomized controlled trial
Does daylight-aligned lighting in intensive care units reduce confusion delirium and length of stay?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that exposure to original artworks or nature-themed images in hospital corridors, waiting rooms, and patient rooms reduces self-reported pain, anxiety, and length of stay?
questions.description: Hospital art evidence — a surprisingly contested area given its widespread implementation.
search_hints: Hospital art corridors patient rooms pain anxiety length of stay evidence
Does artwork in hospitals reduce patient pain and anxiety peer-reviewed evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that access to secure outdoor gardens or indoor horticultural programmes in psychiatric inpatient units reduces agitation episodes, seclusion use, and psychotropic medication requirements?
questions.description: Green space in psychiatric settings — important for both patient safety and biophilic design advocacy.
search_hints: Psychiatric hospital garden access agitation seclusion medication reduction evidence
Do outdoor gardens in psychiatric inpatient units reduce agitation and medication use evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that acoustic redesign of neonatal intensive care units—including sound attenuation, family-centred quiet periods, and exposure to recorded maternal voice—affects neurological development and discharge outcomes in preterm infants born before 32 weeks gestation?
questions.description: Sound environment and neurodevelopment in the most vulnerable population.
search_hints: NICU acoustic design noise reduction preterm infant neurodevelopment discharge outcomes evidence
Does sound attenuation in neonatal ICU improve neurological outcomes in premature babies?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that domestic-scale, nature-integrated dementia care environments—characterised by familiar room layouts, distinctive colour coding, secure garden access, and reduced institutional features—reduce agitation, wandering, and physical restraint use compared to conventional memory care wards?
questions.description: Dementia design evidence — one of the strongest applied cases for K-ATLAS biophilic claims.
search_hints: Dementia care environment design agitation wandering reduction domestic scale evidence
Does nature-integrated dementia ward design reduce agitation and improve quality of life evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that wayfinding design failures in hospitals—including poor signage systems, confusing spatial sequences, and identical-looking corridors—increase patient anxiety, missed appointments, and staff time spent giving directions?
questions.description: Wayfinding anxiety in healthcare — a tangible cost that motivates spatial legibility investment.
search_hints: Hospital wayfinding signage patient anxiety missed appointments spatial complexity evidence
How does poor hospital wayfinding affect patient stress and operational efficiency evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that lighting quality in operating theatres—including colour rendering accuracy, illuminance level uniformity, and surgeon-controlled dimming—affects surgical precision, fatigue, and error rates in complex procedures?
questions.description: Surgeon performance and theatre design — high-stakes application of lighting science.
search_hints: Operating theatre lighting quality surgeon performance fatigue error rate evidence
How does surgical lighting design affect operative precision and surgeon fatigue peer-reviewed studies?

questions.question_text: Do indoor plants measurably improve air quality or cognitive performance, or do effects stem primarily from visual and psychological pathways?
questions.description: Tests two competing explanations for the plant-wellbeing relationship.
search_hints: indoor plants air quality evidence
houseplants cognition
plant volatile organic compounds wellbeing

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that dynamic or daylight-aligned lighting in workplaces improves sleep quality, alertness, or mood in non-clinical populations?
questions.description: Circadian lighting — a rapidly growing evidence base relevant to workplace and school design.
search_hints: dynamic lighting circadian workplace
daylight spectrum alertness
melanopic light sleep quality

questions.question_text: How does colour temperature (warm vs. cool white light) affect alertness, mood, or productivity in educational or office settings?
questions.description: Accessible, practical, and well-studied question with immediate design relevance.
search_hints: colour temperature alertness office
warm cool light mood
CCT cognitive performance classroom

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the visual presence of exposed timber—including wood-grain ceilings, timber-clad walls, and structural wood frames—in educational or healthcare environments produces lower blood pressure, lower cortisol, and higher reported comfort compared to equivalent spaces finished in steel, glass, or concrete?
questions.description: Wood and stress — a growing literature driven partly by mass-timber construction advocates.
search_hints: Exposed timber wood interior cortisol blood pressure comfort wellbeing evidence classroom office
Does visible timber in building interiors reduce stress compared to concrete steel glass finishes evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence from experimental aesthetics and neuroarchitecture shows that curved architectural forms—in facades, room profiles, and furniture—are preferred over sharply angular forms, and what neural or evolutionary mechanism explains this preference?
questions.description: The Damasio curvature preference — widely cited but the mechanism is disputed.
search_hints: Curved versus angular architecture preference neural mechanism experimental aesthetics evidence
Do people prefer curved over angular architectural forms and what explains this curvature preference evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that prospect-conferring architectural elements—including elevated platforms, interior mezzanines, panoramic glazing, and long view corridors—activate approach-related motivation and increase reported preference for and dwell time in a space?
questions.description: Appleton's prospect theory at the interior scale — a gap between landscape preference and architectural preference.
search_hints: Prospect architectural elements mezzanine panoramic view preference approach motivation evidence
Do elevated views and prospect features in buildings increase preference and dwell time evidence?

questions.question_text: What do neuroimaging studies using fMRI reveal about amygdala reactivity when healthy adults view natural landscapes versus urban scenes, and does reduced amygdala activation explain the stress-buffering effect of nature exposure?
questions.description: Direct neural mechanism for SRT's claim that nature reduces threat appraisal. Updating IC→DC for SRT's safety-signalling warrant.
search_hints: fMRI studies amygdala activation nature vs urban scenes stress reduction
Neuroimaging evidence that natural environments reduce amygdala reactivity compared to urban environments

questions.question_text: What neuroimaging evidence shows that viewing natural environments modulates default mode network activity—including self-referential processing and mind-wandering—and how does this relate to the restorative effects described by Attention Restoration Theory?
questions.description: DMN-ART link: the neural mechanism underlying 'being away' and soft fascination in ART.
search_hints: Default mode network activity natural environments restoration neuroimaging
Does nature exposure change default mode network self-referential processing fMRI?

questions.question_text: What evidence from cognitive neuroscience shows that sustained exposure to urban noise—traffic, construction, and crowd sounds—impairs prefrontal cortex-dependent functions including executive attention, inhibitory control, and working memory capacity?
questions.description: Neural mechanism for why urban noise drains ART resources — connects acoustic environment to directed attention via PFC.
search_hints: Urban noise exposure prefrontal cortex working memory cognitive neuroscience evidence
Does traffic noise impair executive attention through prefrontal cortex mechanisms?

questions.question_text: What EEG studies demonstrate that biophilic design features—including natural light, indoor plants, and water sounds—increase posterior alpha-band activity (8–13 Hz), an index of relaxed attentiveness, compared to conventional built environments?
questions.description: EEG-level evidence for biophilic design's relaxation claim. Alpha waves are an established psychophysiology marker.
search_hints: EEG alpha waves biophilic design natural environments relaxation studies
Do indoor plants or nature sounds increase alpha brain wave activity EEG research?

questions.question_text: What evidence from autonomic neuroscience shows that nature exposure specifically activates the parasympathetic nervous system—as measured by high-frequency heart rate variability (HRV)—and what dose of nature (duration, intensity) is required to produce this effect?
questions.description: HRV is the best non-invasive index of parasympathetic tone. Dose-response question is critical for design guidelines.
search_hints: Heart rate variability parasympathetic activation nature exposure dose-response evidence
How much nature exposure is needed to increase heart rate variability HRV systematically review?

questions.question_text: What neuroimaging and cognitive evidence shows that the spatial layout of buildings—including corridor complexity, landmark placement, and room-to-room connectivity—affects hippocampal-dependent spatial memory consolidation and navigation performance?
questions.description: Connects architectural design to hippocampal function — wayfinding stress mechanism at the neural level.
search_hints: Hippocampal spatial memory building layout navigation cognitive neuroscience
How does building complexity affect hippocampal activation and spatial memory in neuroimaging studies?

questions.question_text: What neuroimaging or psychophysiological evidence supports the hypothesis that fractal visual patterns at the preferred complexity range (D=1.3–1.5) activate midline cortical reward circuitry and reduce autonomic arousal compared to high-complexity or low-complexity patterns?
questions.description: Neural mechanism for the Taylor fractal stress-reduction claim — the IC for this claim needs neural evidence to raise DC.
search_hints: Fractal visual patterns reward neural activation psychophysiology fMRI
Do fractal patterns at optimal complexity D=1.3 activate reward circuits and reduce stress neuroimaging?

questions.question_text: What empirical evidence tests the embodied cognition prediction that high-ceiling architectural spaces promote abstract and categorical thinking—via expanded attentional scope—while low-ceiling spaces promote concrete and detail-focused cognition?
questions.description: Meyers-Levy & Zhu (2007) ceiling height effect — needs neural or cognitive replication.
search_hints: Does ceiling height affect abstract vs concrete thinking embodied cognition experimental evidence?
Ceiling height and cognitive flexibility abstract thinking replication study evidence

questions.question_text: What evidence from affective neuroscience and empirical aesthetics shows that architecturally sublime spaces—characterised by vastness, light, and complexity—elicit awe responses with measurable physiological and neural correlates, including changes in default mode network activity and pro-social cognition?
questions.description: Awe in architecture — connects aesthetic theory to the neuroscience of self-transcendence.
search_hints: Neuroimaging awe architectural spaces vast light default mode network evidence
What neural correlates of awe are activated by large cathedral-like or sublime architectural spaces?

questions.question_text: What neuroscientific evidence shows that urban crowding—measured by population density, noise level, and social density within a space—activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis differently from natural crowding such as in forests or parks, and what design interventions attenuate this activation?
questions.description: HPA axis activation as the mechanism for urban stress — distinguishes social from ecological crowding.
search_hints: HPA axis cortisol activation urban crowding versus natural environments neuroscience evidence
Does urban density activate stress hormones through different mechanisms than natural crowding?

questions.question_text: What portable EEG studies conducted in real-world architectural environments—using mobile brain-body imaging—reveal about the relationship between spatial enclosure, ceiling height, light quality, and emotional valence as indexed by frontal alpha asymmetry?
questions.description: Mobile EEG in real buildings — the most ecologically valid neural evidence for environmental psychology.
search_hints: Mobile EEG portable brain imaging built environment emotional response frontal asymmetry
What do real-world EEG studies walking through buildings show about emotional responses to architectural space?

questions.question_text: What neuroimaging evidence supports the hypothesis that the brain uses embodied simulation—activating somatosensory and motor cortices—when visually perceiving architectural surfaces, textures, and spatial enclosure, and does this predict aesthetic preference or aversive responses?
questions.description: Embodied simulation in architectural perception — links mirror neuron system to aesthetic response.
search_hints: Embodied simulation somatosensory cortex architectural perception texture space neuroimaging
Does visual perception of building materials and space activate motor or somatosensory cortex evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence from stress neuroscience links urban environmental stressors—including noise, crowding, and visual complexity—to locus coeruleus-norepinephrine system activation, and how does this explain the cognitive fatigue associated with urban living?
questions.description: LC-NE system as the arousal mechanism for urban stress and cognitive fatigue — bridges SRT and ART at the neural level.
search_hints: Locus coeruleus norepinephrine activation urban stress cognitive fatigue neuroscience
How does urban noise and visual complexity activate the norepinephrine arousal system and cause mental fatigue?

questions.question_text: What evidence from circadian neuroscience shows that morning blue-spectrum light exposure in buildings activates the suprachiasmatic nucleus and the retinohypothalamic tract in ways that improve afternoon cortical arousal, mood, and executive performance?
questions.description: Neural pathway from building daylighting to cognitive performance — SCN → cortisol → PFC.
search_hints: Morning blue light suprachiasmatic nucleus circadian cortical arousal afternoon performance evidence
How does morning light exposure in buildings affect the circadian system and cognitive performance neuroscience?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that streetscapes with historical continuity—characterised by architectural diversity across periods, vernacular character, and preserved original facades—predict residents' place attachment, community identity, and neighbourhood satisfaction more strongly than uniform contemporary streetscapes?
questions.description: Heritage conservation and wellbeing — the psychological cost of erasing historical character.
search_hints: Historical streetscape architectural heritage place attachment community identity satisfaction evidence
Does architectural historical continuity in streetscapes predict resident place attachment evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that forced relocation due to urban redevelopment or demolition of longstanding neighbourhoods produces measurable psychological distress—including grief, depression, and disrupted social identity—in displaced long-term residents, and what aspects of replacement housing design mitigate these effects?
questions.description: Place loss and psychological grief — the social cost of regeneration.
search_hints: Forced relocation urban demolition psychological distress grief depression evidence
What evidence shows psychological harm from forced relocation due to urban redevelopment?

questions.question_text: What evidence tests Kevin Lynch's theoretical framework of city imageability—paths, edges, districts, nodes, and landmarks—by demonstrating that more legible cities, as scored by these elements, produce lower wayfinding stress, faster orientation, and higher resident satisfaction?
questions.description: Lynch's Image of the City tested empirically — influential but lacking rigorous empirical tests.
search_hints: Kevin Lynch city imageability legibility paths nodes landmarks wayfinding satisfaction evidence
What empirical evidence tests Kevin Lynch's urban legibility theory with navigation or stress outcomes?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that urban monuments, public sculptures, and community murals affect collective memory consolidation, civic identity, and emotional wellbeing outcomes in surrounding communities, and do effects differ between communities that participated in commissioning the work versus those that did not?
questions.description: Public art and community wellbeing — participation and co-production may be the active ingredient.
search_hints: Public art monuments collective memory civic identity community wellbeing evidence
Do urban public artworks and monuments improve community wellbeing and civic identity evidence?

questions.question_text: How well do post-occupancy evaluation (POE) studies control for self-selection bias and social desirability when measuring wellbeing in biophilic buildings?
questions.description: Methodological question — tests the quality of the evidentiary base for biophilic design claims.
search_hints: post-occupancy evaluation methodology
biophilic building POE
self-selection wellbeing workspace bias

questions.question_text: How well have the foundational environmental psychology findings of Ulrich (1984) and Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) replicated with larger or more diverse samples?
questions.description: Replication and robustness question — encourages critical appraisal of the field's evidentiary base.
search_hints: Ulrich replication window view
Kaplan ART replication
environmental psychology replication crisis

questions.question_text: What evidence demonstrates the criterion validity and ecological validity of ecological momentary assessment (EMA) methods—using smartphone prompts to capture real-time wellbeing—for measuring environmental psychology responses to built environment features in naturalistic field settings?
questions.description: EMA validation for built environment research — a methodological question with broad implications.
search_hints: Ecological momentary assessment EMA validity built environment wellbeing field studies evidence
Is ecological momentary assessment a valid method for measuring wellbeing responses to built environments?

questions.question_text: What evidence demonstrates that wearable physiological sensors—including actigraphy, photoplethysmography (PPG), and electrodermal activity (EDA) monitors—produce valid and reliable measures of stress in real-world built environment studies compared to laboratory gold standards?
questions.description: Wearable sensor validation — essential as the field moves toward ambulatory measurement.
search_hints: Wearable sensors PPG EDA stress validity reliability built environment real world evidence
How valid are wearable EDA and heart rate sensors for measuring stress compared to lab gold standards?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that virtual reality simulations of built environments produce psychophysiological responses—cortisol, skin conductance, heart rate—comparable to those in matched real-world conditions, validating VR as an experimental platform for architectural and environmental psychology research?
questions.description: VR ecological validity for environmental psychology — key for scaling up controlled studies.
search_hints: Virtual reality built environment psychophysiology validity cortisol skin conductance evidence
Do VR simulations of buildings produce the same physiological stress responses as real buildings evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that eye-tracking measures—including fixation duration, gaze path, and pupillary dilation—in architectural preference studies predict subsequent wellbeing responses, stated preference ratings, and environmental satisfaction independently of self-report measures?
questions.description: Eye-tracking as an implicit preference measure in architecture — does fixation predict preference?
search_hints: Eye tracking fixation architectural preference wellbeing prediction evidence
Does eye tracking gaze data predict architectural preference and wellbeing independently of self-report?

questions.question_text: What evidence demonstrates that sketch-map and cognitive mapping tasks reliably measure the psychological legibility of built environments, and do higher legibility scores on these tasks predict lower navigational anxiety, faster route completion, and higher environmental satisfaction?
questions.description: Cognitive mapping as a legibility measure — bridges Lynch's qualitative framework and quantitative methods.
search_hints: Cognitive mapping sketch map legibility environmental satisfaction navigation anxiety evidence
Do cognitive map tasks validly measure how easy buildings are to navigate and predict anxiety evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence of publication bias exists in the environmental psychology literature on biophilic design—specifically, are positive effects of nature and biophilic design more likely to be published than null or negative results, and how does this bias affect meta-analytic effect size estimates?
questions.description: Publication bias in the biophilic design literature — crucial for calibrating K-ATLAS credences.
search_hints: Publication bias biophilic design environmental psychology nature studies evidence funnel plot
Is there publication bias in nature and biophilic design research that inflates reported effect sizes?

questions.question_text: What do meta-analyses reveal about typical effect sizes for biophilic and nature-based environmental interventions on standardised outcome measures of stress (cortisol, blood pressure), attention (ADHD rating scales, sustained attention tests), and mood (PANAS, DASS)?
questions.description: Effect size calibration across the field — what counts as a clinically or practically significant effect.
search_hints: Effect size meta-analysis biophilic design nature interventions cortisol attention mood standardised
What are the typical effect sizes for nature-based interventions on stress attention and mood meta-analysis?

questions.question_text: What is the track record of pre-registered replication attempts of key findings in environmental psychology—including the attention restoration effects of nature walks, the window view hospital recovery study, and classroom daylighting achievement studies—and what does this reveal about the robustness of the field?
questions.description: Replication and open science in environmental psychology — the meta-scientific health of the field.
search_hints: Pre-registered replication environmental psychology attention restoration window view classroom light evidence
How well have the most cited environmental psychology findings replicated in pre-registered studies?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that ecological quality of urban green spaces—measured by plant species richness, insect biodiversity, and soundscape naturalness—predicts wellbeing and restoration outcomes more strongly than visual greenery alone, and what mechanisms explain this richness effect?
questions.description: Biodiversity and wellbeing — an important question for green space management beyond greenery coverage.
search_hints: Biodiversity species richness urban green space wellbeing restoration evidence mechanism
Does ecological richness and biodiversity in parks produce greater wellbeing than simple greenery area evidence?

questions.question_text: What epidemiological evidence, controlling for socioeconomic status, links residential density, building height, and proximity to green space to mental health outcomes including depression, anxiety, and loneliness in urban residents?
questions.description: Density and mental health — one of the most important policy questions in urban design.
search_hints: Residential density building height mental health depression anxiety evidence controlling socioeconomic
Does living in high-density urban housing increase risk of depression and anxiety evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that intentional co-housing designs—incorporating shared kitchens, living rooms, and gardens—improve social cohesion, reduce loneliness, and lower healthcare utilisation compared to conventional apartment living?
questions.description: Co-housing as an antidote to urban loneliness — an emerging evidence base.
search_hints: Co-housing intentional community shared spaces loneliness social cohesion evidence
Does co-housing design with shared facilities reduce loneliness compared to conventional apartments evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that access to private outdoor space—including gardens, balconies, and roof terraces—buffers the mental health effects of urban density and indoor confinement, particularly during periods of enforced social isolation such as the COVID-19 pandemic?
questions.description: Private green space and mental health resilience — dramatically highlighted by COVID-19 lockdowns.
search_hints: Private garden balcony mental health urban density confinement COVID-19 evidence
Does access to private outdoor space protect mental health during urban confinement and lockdown evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that high-rise residential living—particularly above the 10th floor and without green space access—is independently associated with reduced physical activity, increased loneliness, and poorer mental health outcomes after adjusting for income and household composition?
questions.description: High-rise living and wellbeing — contested since the 1970s housing debates.
search_hints: High-rise apartment living mental health physical activity loneliness evidence
Is living in high-rise buildings associated with worse mental health outcomes evidence independent of income?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the ability to individually control indoor temperature in residential settings—through operable windows, personal thermostats, or ceiling fans—improves sleep quality, morning alertness, and self-reported thermal comfort compared to centrally controlled HVAC systems?
questions.description: Personal control over thermal environment and sleep — connects autonomy, circadian health, and building systems.
search_hints: Individual temperature control residential sleep quality thermal comfort evidence
Does ability to control indoor temperature personally improve sleep quality evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that multisensory environmental coherence—in which visual biophilic elements, natural sounds, natural scents, and thermal conditions reinforce a consistent nature theme—produces stronger restoration and stress reduction than any single sensory channel alone?
questions.description: Multisensory integration and ART/SRT — tests whether sensory cross-modal reinforcement produces superadditive effects.
search_hints: Multisensory coherent environment combined nature sounds scents visuals restoration evidence
Is a multisensory biophilic environment more restorative than single-channel nature stimuli evidence?

questions.question_text: What experimental evidence shows that forest-derived olfactory compounds—specifically phytoncides such as alpha-pinene, limonene, and cedar oil—reduce salivary cortisol, lower blood pressure, and activate the parasympathetic nervous system when diffused in indoor environments?
questions.description: Phytoncides as the active ingredient in shinrin-yoku — important for understanding transferability to built interiors.
search_hints: Phytoncides alpha-pinene forest scent cortisol blood pressure indoor stress reduction evidence
Do forest phytoncide scents diffused indoors reduce stress and cortisol evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence from experimental aesthetics and environmental psychology shows that haptic qualities of building surfaces—including the thermal warmth of wood versus metal, the texture of stone, and the compliance of natural fabric—produce measurable differences in affective responses and perceived environmental quality?
questions.description: Haptic architecture — a neglected sensory channel relative to vision and sound.
search_hints: Haptic surface texture wood metal stone affective response architectural quality evidence
Do building material textures wood versus metal affect emotional responses evidence experimental?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that visual exposure to natural material textures—including wood grain, stone variation, woven natural textiles, and moss surfaces—produces lower electrodermal arousal and higher preference ratings than synthetic materials with uniform, non-fractal surfaces?
questions.description: Visual texture perception and the nature-synthetic divide — extends fractal complexity research to materials.
search_hints: Natural material texture wood grain visual preference arousal EDA evidence comparison synthetic
Do natural textures like wood grain produce lower arousal than synthetic uniform surfaces evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that reverberation time (RT60) in architectural spaces affects not only speech intelligibility and concentration but also perceived spatial size, emotional valence, and the sense of awe in larger reverberant spaces such as cathedrals and concert halls?
questions.description: Reverberation beyond intelligibility — the emotional and aesthetic dimensions of room acoustics.
search_hints: Reverberation RT60 spaciousness emotional response awe architectural acoustics evidence
How does reverberation time affect emotional experience and perceived spaciousness in buildings?

questions.question_text: What design features of indoor common spaces—such as lobbies, courtyards, and shared kitchens—promote spontaneous social interaction among building occupants, and what peer-reviewed evidence links increased interaction to wellbeing outcomes?
questions.description: Design for social contact — connects physical affordances to loneliness and cohesion outcomes.
search_hints: What design features in shared indoor spaces promote spontaneous social interaction?
How does common-space design in office or residential buildings affect social cohesion and wellbeing?

questions.question_text: What peer-reviewed evidence shows that the degree of spatial enclosure in public plazas and urban squares affects the number, duration, and quality of spontaneous social encounters between strangers?
questions.description: Tests William H. Whyte's social life of small urban spaces empirically.
search_hints: What evidence links spatial enclosure in public plazas to frequency of social encounters?
How does street and plaza design affect spontaneous social interaction in urban spaces peer-reviewed studies?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that biophilic features in public spaces—including water elements, seating near trees, and planted courtyards—increase dwell time and incidental social contact compared to unplanted hardscape spaces?
questions.description: Links biophilic design to social affordance — important because biophilia is usually measured individually.
search_hints: Do biophilic design features in public spaces increase social interaction and dwell time?
Evidence that green spaces and water features in urban plazas promote social contact

questions.question_text: What does peer-reviewed research show about the effect of shared communal gardens in residential apartment buildings on social cohesion, loneliness, and mental health in urban dwellers?
questions.description: Communal gardens as social infrastructure — fills a gap between horticultural therapy and environmental psychology.
search_hints: Does access to communal gardens in apartment buildings reduce loneliness and improve mental health?
Peer-reviewed evidence on shared residential gardens and social cohesion in urban housing

questions.question_text: What evidence links the absence or poor quality of third-place spaces—such as coffee shops, libraries, and community parks—in built environments to increased social isolation and mental health difficulties, particularly in car-dependent neighbourhoods?
questions.description: Oldenburg's third place theory applied to mental health — empirical test of the planning-isolation hypothesis.
search_hints: How does the absence of third-place spaces in suburban or car-dependent areas affect social isolation?
Evidence linking lack of community spaces libraries parks to loneliness mental health

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that physical proximity—desk-sharing, open adjacency, and collocation—between employees in knowledge-work organisations increases spontaneous communication, cross-team learning, and creative output?
questions.description: The MIT Building 20 hypothesis — physical proximity drives intellectual serendipity.
search_hints: Does physical proximity between workers increase collaboration and creative output evidence?
How does office layout and desk proximity affect spontaneous knowledge sharing in organisations?

questions.question_text: How does building spatial complexity and legibility affect wayfinding-related stress and cognitive load in hospital or campus environments?
questions.description: Tests spatial navigation theory — hippocampal mapping and environmental clarity predictions.
search_hints: wayfinding stress hospital
building legibility cognitive load
spatial complexity stress navigation

questions.question_text: What levels of architectural visual complexity do people prefer, and how does preference relate to environmental familiarity or cultural background?
questions.description: Berlyne / Wohlwill optimal complexity theory applied to architecture.
search_hints: optimal complexity preference architecture
Berlyne arousal aesthetic
architectural complexity preference cross-cultural

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that sensory-modulated architectural design—including reduced luminance contrast, matte non-reflective surfaces, acoustic dampening, predictable spatial sequences, and neutral colour palettes—improves behavioural outcomes and reduces meltdown frequency in children with autism spectrum disorder?
questions.description: ASD-inclusive design evidence — a growing field with strong clinical implications.
search_hints: Autism spectrum disorder sensory architecture design meltdown behavioural outcomes evidence
Does sensory-modulated building design reduce meltdowns and improve behaviour in children with autism?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that biophilic interventions in memory care units—including living plants, aquaria, bird feeders, and access to secured gardens—specifically reduce agitation episodes and antipsychotic medication use in residents with moderate to severe dementia?
questions.description: Biophilic design in dementia care — a high-value clinical application with medication-reduction implications.
search_hints: Biophilic design dementia memory care agitation antipsychotic medication reduction evidence plants aquarium
Do plants, aquaria and gardens in dementia care units reduce agitation and drug use evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that older adults (65+) in care or residential settings with access to maintained outdoor gardens or nature views experience lower rates of depression, lower fall incidence, and higher self-reported quality of life compared to those in comparable settings without nature access?
questions.description: Nature access and healthy ageing — connects biophilic design to evidence-based care.
search_hints: Older adults garden access residential care depression falls quality of life evidence
Does nature access in care homes for elderly people reduce depression and improve quality of life?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that classroom acoustic design—including sound-field amplification systems, lower reverberation ceilings, and reduced background noise—differentially benefits children with hearing impairment, auditory processing disorder, or English as a second language?
questions.description: Acoustic equity in classrooms — the intersection of design and disability justice.
search_hints: Classroom acoustic design hearing impairment auditory processing sound field amplification evidence
Do acoustic improvements in classrooms benefit children with hearing impairment more than typical peers?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that physical environment features in palliative care settings—including single rooms, family accommodation, access to outdoor views, and patient control of light and sound—are associated with higher quality end-of-life experience as rated by patients and bereaved families?
questions.description: End-of-life environment quality — evidence base for hospice design.
search_hints: Palliative care environment design quality end of life experience family satisfaction evidence
Does hospice or palliative care building design affect patient experience and family-rated quality of dying?

questions.question_text: What physiological markers (cortisol, heart rate, skin conductance) show recovery after exposure to natural versus urban environments?
questions.description: Tests Ulrich (1983) SRT with objective measures. Well-represented in the literature.
search_hints: Ulrich stress recovery nature
cortisol nature exposure
physiological stress urban nature

questions.question_text: What evidence directly tests the hypothesis that safety signalling — rather than mere pleasantness — explains why savanna-like landscapes reduce stress?
questions.description: Tests the evolutionary safety-signal mechanism in SRT. Sparse direct evidence; good gap question.
search_hints: savanna hypothesis stress
prospect refuge theory Appleton
evolutionary landscape preference safety

questions.question_text: Are Attention Restoration Theory, Stress Recovery Theory, and the Biophilia Hypothesis three distinct theories or do they converge on the same underlying mechanism?
questions.description: Meta-theoretical question — encourages students to think about explanatory architecture and theoretical redundancy.
search_hints: ART SRT biophilia unified
nature theories comparison mechanism
restorative environment theories synthesis

questions.question_text: What evidence directly compares the magnitude of restorative and stress-reducing effects between authentic natural environments—parks, forests, gardens—and high-quality built biophilic simulations of equivalent sensory complexity, using matched physiological and self-report outcome measures?
questions.description: Real nature vs. biophilic design — the dose equivalence question for design guidelines.
search_hints: Real nature versus biophilic design restorative effects comparison magnitude evidence
How does the stress reduction from biophilic building design compare to real nature exposure evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that individual differences—including trait anxiety, nature connectedness scores, urban versus rural upbringing, and sensory processing sensitivity—moderate the magnitude of biophilic and restorative design effects, and should design standards account for such variability?
questions.description: Individual moderators of environmental psychology effects — critical for moving from mean effects to personalised design.
search_hints: Individual differences nature connectedness trait anxiety moderate biophilic restoration evidence
Do personality and nature connectedness traits moderate how much people benefit from biophilic design evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence characterises dose-response relationships in environmental psychology—specifically, what minimum amount or quality of nature (duration of exposure, proximity, species richness, canopy cover) is required to produce statistically and clinically significant reductions in cortisol or improvements in attention?
questions.description: Dose-response question — essential for translating evidence into actionable design thresholds.
search_hints: Nature exposure dose response cortisol attention minimum duration quality evidence
What is the minimum nature exposure duration or quality needed for measurable stress reduction evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence from post-occupancy evaluations of buildings certified under the WELL Building Standard, LEED, or the Living Building Challenge shows that certified buildings actually achieve their predicted health, wellbeing, and performance outcomes compared to non-certified counterparts?
questions.description: Green building certification and actual outcomes — a gap between design intent and measured effect.
search_hints: WELL Building Standard LEED certification post-occupancy health outcomes evidence
Do WELL or LEED certified buildings actually improve occupant health and performance post-occupancy evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that buildings widely considered aesthetically aversive or institutionally oppressive—including brutalist housing blocks, windowless offices, and legacy public housing estates—produce measurable negative psychological effects independent of the material conditions they provide?
questions.description: Aesthetic aversion and wellbeing — distinguishes the visual experience of architecture from functional adequacy.
search_hints: Aesthetically aversive architecture brutalist housing psychological wellbeing negative effects evidence
Do ugly or aesthetically oppressive buildings cause psychological harm independent of physical conditions evidence?

questions.question_text: What is the optimal temperature range for cognitive performance in office settings, and how much do deviations above or below this range impair output?
questions.description: Classic indoor environment quality question with practical design implications.
search_hints: temperature cognitive performance office
thermal comfort productivity
ASHRAE 55 cognitive impairment

questions.question_text: What epidemiological evidence links residential proximity to urban green spaces with better mental health outcomes?
questions.description: Population-level green space health question — large epidemiological literature.
search_hints: urban green space mental health
park proximity anxiety depression
greenery residential health outcomes

questions.question_text: How does access to green space affect cognitive development and sustained attention in children, particularly those diagnosed with ADHD?
questions.description: Children, ADHD, and green space — a strong sub-literature with policy implications.
search_hints: green space children attention
ADHD nature exposure
outdoor play cognitive development

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that neighbourhood-level urban tree canopy cover—at density thresholds above 20–30%—measurably reduces ambient surface temperatures, reduces perceived heat stress in pedestrians, and lowers indoor cooling energy demand in buildings below the canopy?
questions.description: Urban tree canopy density thresholds — design guidance needs quantitative evidence.
search_hints: Urban tree canopy cover threshold temperature reduction heat stress pedestrian evidence
What percentage of urban tree canopy is needed to measurably reduce heat stress and temperature?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that exterior living walls and green facades on urban buildings produce net benefits across thermal insulation, urban biodiversity, and the wellbeing of building occupants who can view them, after accounting for maintenance costs and irrigation requirements?
questions.description: Green facades — multi-criteria evidence synthesis needed for design decisions.
search_hints: Living wall green facade building thermal performance biodiversity occupant wellbeing evidence
Do exterior green walls on buildings reduce energy use and improve occupant wellbeing net benefit evidence?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that proximity to urban water bodies—rivers, canals, coastal shorelines, and urban fountains—independently predicts higher physical activity, lower stress, and better mental health in nearby residents, beyond what is explained by general green space access?
questions.description: Blue space effect independent of green space — an important distinction for urban planning.
search_hints: Blue space urban water proximity physical activity mental health independent green space evidence
Does living near urban water bodies improve mental health independently of access to green spaces?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that street lighting quality—including illuminance level, colour rendering, uniformity, and spectral composition—affects pedestrian-perceived safety, fear of crime, and actual pedestrian injury or assault rates in urban environments?
questions.description: Street lighting and public safety — evidence for CPTED and night-time economy design.
search_hints: Street lighting quality safety fear crime pedestrian injury evidence urban
How does street lighting level and quality affect actual crime rates and fear of crime evidence?

questions.question_text: What empirical evidence supports the predictions of Jan Gehl's urban design principles—including human scale, active building frontages, and permeability—for pedestrian density, dwell time, spontaneous social interaction, and perceived street quality?
questions.description: Gehl's design principles tested empirically — influential but surprisingly few controlled studies.
search_hints: Jan Gehl urban design principles pedestrian density dwell time social interaction evidence
What empirical evidence tests Jan Gehl's human scale active frontage principles for pedestrian activity?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that hospital patients with window views of nature recover faster or report lower pain than those with views of walls or urban scenes?
questions.description: Classic Ulrich (1984) window view study and subsequent replication attempts.
search_hints: Ulrich window view hospital recovery
nature view patient outcomes
hospital design nature window

questions.question_text: Do virtual or simulated nature views (screens, VR, projected scenes) produce restorative effects comparable to real windows onto nature?
questions.description: Important for healthcare and office design where real windows are not possible.
search_hints: virtual nature restorative
nature video stress recovery
simulated window view wellbeing VR

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that water features (fountains, indoor water walls, water sounds) reduce stress or improve wellbeing in built environments?
questions.description: Blue space research — an emerging literature complementing green space findings.
search_hints: blue space wellbeing
water feature stress fountain
aquatic environment restoration indoor

questions.question_text: What peer-reviewed evidence shows that activity-based working environments—where employees choose their workspace according to task type rather than having assigned desks—improve job satisfaction, cross-team collaboration, and productivity compared to conventional assigned-desk offices?
questions.description: ABW is now ubiquitous in corporate real estate but the evidence base is contested.
search_hints: Activity-based working hot desking productivity satisfaction collaboration evidence systematic review
Does activity-based working improve employee wellbeing and productivity compared to assigned desks?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the provision of designated quiet zones and fully enclosed private focus spaces in open-plan offices reduces noise-related distraction and improves self-reported deep-work performance for knowledge workers?
questions.description: Quiet zones in open-plan offices — a partial mitigation strategy with growing evidence.
search_hints: Quiet zones private focus rooms open plan office distraction productivity evidence
Do quiet private spaces in open plan offices reduce noise distraction for knowledge workers?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that restorative break environments within workplace buildings—including access to courtyard gardens, nature-view lounges, or quiet green spaces—reduce afternoon cognitive fatigue and improve sustained attention performance compared to break rooms without nature access?
questions.description: Break room design and recovery — links ART micro-restoration to afternoon work performance.
search_hints: Workplace break room garden nature view cognitive fatigue recovery afternoon performance evidence
Do nature-integrated office break spaces reduce afternoon fatigue compared to conventional break rooms?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that the ability to personalise one's workspace—by displaying personal photographs, plants, or decorative objects—affects employees' psychological ownership, job satisfaction, and organisational commitment, and do these effects differ between hot-desking and assigned-desk conditions?
questions.description: Personalisation and ownership — a psychological loss associated with activity-based working.
search_hints: Workspace personalisation psychological ownership job satisfaction evidence office
Does allowing workers to personalise their desks improve psychological ownership and retention evidence?

questions.question_text: What systematic review or longitudinal evidence supports the claim that the presence of indoor vegetation—living walls, desktop plants, or atrium gardens—in office workplaces reduces employee absenteeism and self-reported wellbeing scores?
questions.description: The absenteeism claim for office plants is widely cited but needs scrutiny.
search_hints: Indoor office plants absenteeism wellbeing evidence systematic review
Do indoor plants in offices measurably reduce absenteeism evidence peer-reviewed?

questions.question_text: What evidence shows that lighting design in deep-plan offices without window access—using high colour rendering index (CRI ≥ 90), tunable white light, and biodynamic lighting systems—compensates for the absence of natural light in terms of mood, alertness, and visual comfort?
questions.description: Artificial light compensation for windowless offices — an important equity and health question.
search_hints: Deep plan office artificial lighting CRI tunable white mood alertness evidence
Can high-quality artificial lighting compensate for lack of natural light in windowless offices evidence?
